messages.content: Is fish safe during pregnancy?
Fish like salmon is safe in moderation for omega-3s, but limit intake to avoid mercury. Avoid raw fish. Consult your doctor.
What about calcium?
Calcium from milk, curd, and leafy greens is vital for your bones and baby’s. Aim for 1000 mg daily as per ICMR.
Can I eat papaya?
Unripe papaya should be avoided due to contraction risks. Ripe papaya is okay in small amounts. Check with your doctor.
What vitamins do I need?
You need folic acid, iron, and vitamin D. Take supplements as prescribed by your health worker.
Is coffee safe?
Limit coffee to 1-2 cups daily to avoid affecting the baby. Avoid if advised by your doctor.
What about iron?
Iron from spinach and liver helps prevent anemia. Take supplements if prescribed by your doctor.
Can I eat spicy food?
Spicy food is generally safe but may cause heartburn. Eat in moderation and consult your doctor if issues arise.
How much water should I drink?
Drink 2-3 liters of water daily to stay hydrated and support amniotic fluid, per ICMR guidelines.
Are nuts safe?
Nuts like almonds are safe and provide healthy fats. Avoid if you have allergies, and consult your doctor.
What about sugar?
Limit sugar to prevent gestational diabetes. Opt for natural sugars from fruits instead.
Can I eat eggs?
Yes, well-cooked eggs are safe and provide protein. Avoid raw eggs to prevent salmonella.
What about dairy?
Dairy like milk and yogurt is essential for calcium. Choose low-fat options if needed.
Is rice okay?
Yes, rice is a good carbohydrate source. Pair with vegetables for a balanced meal.
Can I eat street food?
Avoid street food due to hygiene risks. Opt for home-cooked meals to stay safe.
What about vitamin C?
Vitamin C from oranges and tomatoes boosts immunity. Aim for 85 mg daily during pregnancy.
Is ghee safe?
Ghee is safe in moderation for energy, but avoid excess to prevent weight gain. Consult your doctor.
Can I eat lentils?
Yes, lentils are a great protein source. Cook them well to aid digestion.
What about salt?
Use salt in moderation to avoid swelling. Check with your doctor if you have high blood pressure.
Is juice good?
Fresh juice is good for vitamins, but avoid sugary ones. Limit to 1 glass daily.
Can I eat sweets?
Limit sweets to prevent weight gain and diabetes. Opt for fruit-based desserts.
Is flu shot needed?
Yes, the flu shot is recommended during flu season to reduce complications. Consult your doctor.
When is COVID-19 dose 2?
The second COVID-19 dose is at 28 weeks, per Indian guidelines. Confirm with your health worker.
Is TT safe?
Yes, TT vaccines (TT1 at 12 weeks, TT2 at 20 weeks) are safe and protect against tetanus.
What about hepatitis B?
Hepatitis B is for at-risk women, with doses at 20 and 28 weeks. Consult your doctor.
Is rubella vaccine okay?
No, it’s not given during pregnancy. Take it before conception or postpartum.
When is TT booster due?
A TT booster is due if your last shot was over 3 years ago. Check with your provider.
Is HPV safe during pregnancy?
No, HPV is not recommended during pregnancy. Consider it postpartum.
What about pneumococcal?
It’s for women with chronic conditions, with one dose during pregnancy if needed.
Is varicella safe?
No, varicella is contraindicated during pregnancy. Take it after delivery.
When is influenza shot given?
The influenza shot is given during flu season, ideally before peak months.
Is COVID-19 vaccine necessary?
Yes, it’s safe and recommended at 20 and 28 weeks. Consult your doctor.
What if I miss TT1?
Contact your health worker to reschedule TT1 as soon as possible.
Is booster safe?
Yes, a booster is safe if needed, following the 3-year rule. Confirm with your doctor.
When is hepatitis B dose 2?
The second hepatitis B dose is at 28 weeks for at-risk women.
Is flu shot free?
It may be free under government programs. Check with your local health center.
What about rubella postpartum?
Yes, the rubella vaccine can be given safely after delivery.
Is HPV postpartum okay?
Yes, HPV vaccination is recommended postpartum to prevent cervical cancer.
When is pneumococcal due?
It’s given once during pregnancy for chronic conditions, as advised.
Is varicella postpartum safe?
Yes, the varicella vaccine is safe and recommended after delivery.
What if I miss COVID-19 dose?
Contact your health worker to reschedule your COVID-19 dose promptly.
When is MMR?
MMR is given at 9 months with a booster at 15-18 months per India’s schedule.
What if I miss a dose?
If you miss a dose, consult a health worker to reschedule and maintain immunity.
When is DPT due?
DPT is due at 6, 10, and 14 weeks after birth. Follow up with your provider.
What about rotavirus?
Rotavirus is given at 6, 10, and 14 weeks, with a last dose by 8 months.
When is Hib vaccine?
Hib is administered at 6, 10, and 14 weeks to prevent meningitis.
What about polio?
Polio (OPV) is given at birth, 6, 10, 14 weeks, and a booster at 16-24 months.
When is hepatitis B for baby?
Hepatitis B starts at birth, then at 6 and 14 weeks.
What is PCV schedule?
PCV is given at 6 weeks, 14 weeks, and 9 months to prevent pneumonia.
Is measles vaccine safe?
Yes, the measles vaccine at 9 months is safe and effective.
When is MMR booster?
The MMR booster is due at 15-18 months as per the schedule.
What if my child refuses a shot?
Calm your child and try again, or consult a health worker for assistance.
When is rotavirus last dose?
The last rotavirus dose should be by 8 months of age.
Is Hib important?
Yes, Hib prevents serious infections like meningitis, so it’s crucial.
When is polio booster?
The polio booster is at 16-24 months to ensure immunity.
What about hepatitis B booster?
No routine booster is needed for hepatitis B after the infant series.
Is PCV free?
Yes, PCV is often free under government programs. Check locally.
When is measles booster?
The measles booster is part of MMR at 15-18 months.
What if DPT is delayed?
Contact a health worker to reschedule DPT to maintain protection.
Is rotavirus necessary?
Yes, it’s necessary to prevent severe diarrhea in infants.
When is Hib booster?
There’s no routine Hib booster; the series ends at 14 weeks.
How often for checkups?
Checkups are monthly until 28 weeks, biweekly to 36 weeks, then weekly.
What are danger signs?
Danger signs include severe headache, bleeding, or vision changes. Seek help immediately.
When is ultrasound done?
Ultrasounds are at 8-12 weeks and 18-20 weeks for screening.
What about blood pressure?
Blood pressure checks detect preeclampsia. Regular monitoring is key.
Is weight gain normal?
Yes, 0.5-1 kg per month in the first trimester is normal. Consult your doctor.
What if I urinate often?
Frequent urination is normal due to bladder pressure. See a doctor if painful.
When is glucose test?
Glucose screening is between 24-28 weeks to check for diabetes.
What about swelling?
Mild swelling is normal, but sudden swelling may indicate preeclampsia. Consult a doctor.
Is dizziness common?
Yes, dizziness can occur due to low blood pressure. Rest and hydrate; see a doctor if severe.
What about heartburn?
Heartburn is common due to hormonal changes. Eat small meals and consult your doctor.
When is the next visit?
It depends on your stage—monthly until 28 weeks, then adjust with your doctor.
What if I feel pain?
Severe pain may indicate issues like preeclampsia. Seek medical help immediately.
Is bleeding normal?
Light spotting can be normal, but heavy bleeding is a danger sign. See a doctor.
What about fatigue?
Fatigue is common, especially early on. Rest and eat iron-rich foods.
When is postnatal check?
A postnatal check is recommended within 6 weeks after delivery.
What is postnatal depression?
It’s sadness or sleep issues after delivery. Seek help if it persists.
Is stress normal?
Mild stress is normal, but chronic stress needs management with a doctor.
What about back pain?
Back pain is common due to weight gain. Rest and consult if severe.
When is anemia checked?
Anemia is checked at every prenatal visit, starting from the first trimester.
What if I gain too much weight?
Excess weight gain may need dietary adjustment. Consult your doctor.
When to start solids?
Start solids at 6 months with mashed vegetables or cereals, per WHO guidelines.
Is formula okay?
Formula is okay if breastfeeding isn’t possible. Consult a pediatrician for the right type.
What foods at 6 months?
At 6 months, offer iron-fortified cereals and pureed fruits like apples.
Can I give water?
Yes, introduce water in small sips after 6 months for hydration.
What about meats?
Pureed meats can start after 8 months, well-cooked and salt-free.
Are fruits good?
Yes, fruits like mangoes are good by 9 months for vitamins.
Can I use honey?
No, avoid honey until one year to prevent botulism risk.
What about rice?
Rice porridge is a good first food at 6 months, easy to digest.
Is juice okay?
Fresh juice is okay in small amounts after 6 months, avoiding sugar.
What if my child refuses food?
Try different textures or consult a pediatrician if refusal persists.
When to introduce eggs?
Introduce well-cooked eggs after 9 months, avoiding raw ones.
What about dairy?
Dairy like yogurt can start at 6 months for calcium.
Can I give salt?
No, avoid salt until 12 months to protect kidneys.
What about vegetables?
Mashed vegetables like carrots are ideal from 6 months.
Is breastfeeding enough?
Yes, until 6 months, then add solids while continuing.
What if my child is underweight?
Consult a pediatrician to adjust diet or check health.
When to stop breastfeeding?
Continue up to 2 years, per WHO, with solids after 6 months.
Can I give nuts?
Avoid whole nuts until 5 years; use ground nuts after 1 year.
What about snacks?
Offer healthy snacks like fruit puree after 6 months.
Is sugar okay?
Avoid added sugar until 2 years to prevent health issues.
